description: Declarative tool interface contracts for agentic runtimes — manifest format (.clad.toml) defining typed parameters, validation rules, command templates, output schemas, Cedar policy mappings, risk tiers, and evidence envelopes. Three execution modes: oneshot CLI, interactive PTY session with per-interaction governance, and headless browser via CDP. Reference implementations in Rust, Python, JavaScript, and Go produce byte-identical evidence envelopes. Signs .clad.toml directly with SchemaPin (no [tool.schemapin] section needed); v1.4-alpha additive options recommended for production: --expires-in TTL, --schema-version derived from [tool] version, --previous-hash for lineage chain enforcement, plus _schemapin.{vendor-domain} DNS TXT cross-verification. Part of the ThirdKey trust stack: SchemaPin (tool integrity) → AgentPin (agent identity) → ToolClad (tool contracts) → Symbiont (runtime).
rules: ToolClad inverts the sandbox approach: LLM fills typed parameters constrained by the manifest, executor validates and constructs the command. The LLM never generates or sees shell commands.
All string-based types reject shell metacharacters by default (`;|&$\`(){}[]<>!\n\r`). Array-based execution via execve, no `sh -c` interpretation.
Three dispatch modes: oneshot (default — single command execution), session (PTY with per-interaction Cedar gating), browser (CDP/Playwright with URL scope enforcement and page state policies).
14 built-in type validators: string, integer, number, port, boolean, enum, scope_target, cidr, ip, hostname, url, path, file, regex. All include injection sanitization.

Conditional evaluators in [command.conditionals] use closed-vocabulary parsers — NEVER eval(), Function(), exec(), or dynamic code execution. RCE prevention is a security requirement enforced across all four reference implementations.

Manifests are signed directly with `schemapin-sign tools/<name>.clad.toml`. No `[tool.schemapin]` section required — the manifest stays clean. Verification happens at the runtime layer via SchemaPin's .well-known/schemapin.json discovery.

Recommended SchemaPin v1.4-alpha signing for production manifests: pass `--expires-in 6mo` (forces re-signing cadence; verifiers degrade past expires_at), `--schema-version $TOOL_VERSION` (semver from [tool] version, surfaces as VerificationResult.schema_version for policy use), and `--previous-hash $PREV_SKILL_HASH` for subsequent releases (forms a lineage chain; verifiers pair with verify_chain to defend against rug-pull substitutions).

Recommended DNS TXT publication: vendors SHOULD publish `_schemapin.{vendor-domain}` IN TXT "v=schemapin1; kid=...; fp=sha256:..." alongside .well-known/schemapin.json. Runtimes that adopt cross-verification fail closed on mismatches between HTTPS hosting and DNS channels (DOMAIN_MISMATCH).

Evidence envelope is structured JSON with scan_id, timestamps, exit_code, stderr, output_hash (SHA-256), platform-aware temp directory paths. HTTP error semantics: 4xx → client_error, 5xx → server_error.
Output parsers: builtin:json, builtin:xml, builtin:csv, builtin:jsonl, builtin:text, plus custom scripts. [output.schema] validates parsed results before they reach the LLM.
Cross-language guarantee: Rust, Python, JavaScript, and Go reference implementations produce byte-identical evidence envelopes for the same manifest + arguments. Mandatory.

Part of the ThirdKey trust stack: SchemaPin (tool integrity) → AgentPin (agent identity) → ToolClad (tool contracts) → Symbiont (runtime). ToolClad is the layer that turns SchemaPin-verified contracts into safely dispatched commands.
